title: Know Your Customer
subtitle: KYC, screening, red flags & reporting

hook.metaphor: Before the bouncer lets anyone in, he checks the ID, glances at the 'do-not-admit' list, and keeps an eye out for trouble once they're inside. That's the heart of fighting financial crime: know who you're dealing with at the door (**KYC**), screen them against the banned lists (**sanctions/PEPs**), and watch for odd behaviour afterwards (**monitoring**) — and when something's clearly off, tell the authorities.
hook.scenario: A new corporate customer wants to open an account. The paperwork is vague about who really owns the company, the listed director shares a name with a sanctioned individual, and the firm immediately starts wiring large sums to a high-risk jurisdiction. How many alarms should be ringing?

learn: It starts with **KYC — Know Your Customer** (the deeper process is **Customer Due Diligence, CDD**). You verify *who* the customer really is, and for companies, the **beneficial owner** — the actual human who controls it (criminals love to hide behind opaque corporate layers, as in our scenario).

Risk isn't uniform, so you apply a **risk-based approach**: light-touch checks for low-risk customers, and **Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)** for high-risk ones — including **PEPs (Politically Exposed Persons)**, whose position carries higher bribery/corruption risk, and customers tied to high-risk jurisdictions.

Then **sanctions screening**: checking customers and payments against government 'banned lists' (sanctioned people, entities, countries). A sanctions breach is a strict-liability nightmare — firms face enormous fines even for accidental hits, so screening is non-negotiable.

Finally, **ongoing monitoring** watches transactions for red flags — sudden spikes, round-number transfers, links to risky places, behaviour that doesn't fit the customer's profile. When something is genuinely suspicious, the firm files a report to the national financial-intelligence unit: a **SAR/STR** (Suspicious Activity/Transaction Report) — or in Australia an **SMR** to **AUSTRAC**, with parallels at NZ's FIU and India's **FIU-IND**. A crucial rule: **'tipping off'** — warning the customer they've been reported — is itself a serious crime.
bridge: So at work, you can sanity-check any onboarding or transaction against four questions: *Do we truly know who this is (and who really owns it)? Have we screened them against sanctions/PEP lists? Is their behaviour consistent with their profile? And if it's off, have we reported it — without tipping them off?* Those four cover the backbone of any financial-crime programme.

interactions.title: The suspicious account
interactions.prompt: Vague ownership, a director matching a sanctions name, and instant large wires to a high-risk country. What's the correct action?

options.label: Open it — innocent until proven guilty.
options.feedback: This isn't a court; it's risk management. Multiple serious red flags (hidden ownership + possible sanctions match + high-risk wires) demand EDD and likely a report before proceeding — not a shrug.

options.label: Do enhanced due diligence, screen the sanctions match, and file a SAR if confirmed.
options.feedback: Exactly. Resolve the beneficial owner, clear or confirm the sanctions hit, and if suspicion holds, report to the FIU — quietly. This is the risk-based approach working as designed.

options.label: Quietly warn the customer you're investigating them.
options.feedback: Never. That's 'tipping off' — a criminal offence that sabotages investigations. You report to the authorities, not to the customer.

interactions.title: The KYC toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Verifying who your customer really is
The actual human who ultimately controls a company
A politically exposed person — higher corruption risk
Checking names against government banned lists
A report of suspicious activity filed to the authorities
Illegally warning a customer they've been reported

interactions.title: Standard or enhanced?
interactions.prompt: Which customers warrant Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) versus standard checks?

items.label: A salaried local with a simple savings account
A senior foreign government official (a PEP)
A company with deliberately opaque ownership
A client routing funds through a high-risk jurisdiction

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Fighting financial crime means knowing who you're dealing with (KYC), checking them against the banned lists (sanctions/PEPs), watching for odd behaviour, and quietly reporting it when it's off — never tipping the customer off.”

checkpoint.q: The 'beneficial owner' of a company is…
options: The actual human who ultimately owns or controls it
checkpoint.explain: Criminals hide behind corporate layers, so KYC must identify the real human in control.

checkpoint.q: 'Tipping off' a customer that they've been reported is…
checkpoint.explain: Warning the subject sabotages the investigation and is itself a crime; you report to the FIU, not the customer.

checkpoint.q: A PEP (Politically Exposed Person) typically triggers…
options: Enhanced Due Diligence due to higher corruption risk
checkpoint.explain: Their position carries elevated bribery/corruption risk, so a risk-based approach applies enhanced scrutiny.
